SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: they evaluate across three datasets (English and Portuguese), compare enriched complex-network features to Bag-of-Words and linguistic baselines, and explore classifier ensembles and multi-view learning with reported hyperparameters. The paper also discusses limitations (short texts, OOV words, lemmatization issues) and reportedly has thorough related-work coverage. However, the summary does not indicate dedicated ablation/sensitivity analyses beyond the main feature comparisons, and code availability is not mentioned, which are minor gaps for full reproducibility and deeper robustness assessment. | B(4): The experiments cover multiple datasets (English and Portuguese) and tasks, compare the proposed enriched complex-network features to strong baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and BoW), evaluate multiple classifiers, and explore ensemble/multi-view combinations, which supports the main claims. The paper also explicitly discusses limitations of short texts and OOV words and reports hyperparameters, aiding reproducibility. However, the summary does not indicate dedicated ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., comparing different embedding methods or the contribution of enrichment vs. raw co-occurrence features) nor is code availability mentioned, so some important analyses and reproduction convenience are missing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an NLP/ML task (automatic detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment from speech transcripts) using modeling choices (word-adjacency networks enriched with word embeddings), standard classifiers (SVM, RF, KNN, GaussianNB), and comparisons to established baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and BoW) evaluated on well-known datasets (DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, ABCD). These elements and the multilingual evaluation align well with the audience and expectations of a top NLP conference; the methodology and evaluation standards are appropriate. Minor scope concern: the work is application-heavy/clinical and may be better suited to a top NLP venue than a core ML conference focused on methodological novelty. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML application (automatic detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment from speech transcripts) using methods and evaluation typical for the community. It proposes a concrete modeling approach (word‑adjacency networks enriched with Bojanowski et al. (2016) embeddings), extracts standard topological features, compares against established baselines (Coh‑Metrix / Coh‑Metrix‑Dementia and Bag‑of‑Words), reports hyperparameters, and evaluates on multiple datasets (DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, ABCD/AZ Battery), including cross‑lingual experiments. These attributes indicate strong relevance and appropriate positioning for a top NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an NLP/ML task (automatic detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment from speech transcripts) using modeling choices (word-adjacency networks enriched with word embeddings), standard classifiers (SVM, RF, KNN, GaussianNB), and comparisons to established baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and BoW) evaluated on well-known datasets (DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, ABCD). These elements and the multilingual evaluation align well with the audience and expectations of a top NLP conference; the methodology and evaluation standards are appropriate. Minor scope concern: the work is application-heavy/clinical and may be better suited to a top NLP venue than a core ML conference focused on methodological novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML application (automatic detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment from speech transcripts) using methods and evaluation typical for the community. It proposes a concrete modeling approach (word‑adjacency networks enriched with Bojanowski et al. (2016) embeddings), extracts standard topological features, compares against established baselines (Coh‑Metrix / Coh‑Metrix‑Dementia and Bag‑of‑Words), reports hyperparameters, and evaluates on multiple datasets (DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, ABCD/AZ Battery), including cross‑lingual experiments. These attributes indicate strong relevance and appropriate positioning for a top NLP/ML venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The main novel element is augmenting word-adjacency (co-occurrence) networks with subword-aware word embeddings to add semantic edges and thereby improve topological characterization of short, disfluent assessment texts; applying this enriched-network representation to MCI detection across multiple datasets/languages is a useful, concrete contribution. However, the approach largely composes established components (co-occurrence networks, word embeddings, standard classifiers, ensemble/multi-view learning) into a new pipeline rather than introducing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Given the reported cross-lingual application and handling of OOV via subword embeddings, the work represents a moderate, incremental advance rather than a highly original breakthrough. | B(3): The work combines established components—word-adjacency (co-occurrence) networks, Bojanowski et al. (2016) subword embeddings, standard network topological measures, and common classifiers—but proposes a concrete enrichment of short-text networks with embedding-derived semantic edges to better represent short assessment transcripts for MCI detection. The cross-language evaluation on multiple datasets (DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, and ABCD) and the use of ensemble/multi-view fusion are useful and non-trivial extensions, but they largely integrate existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamentally new method or paradigm. Overall, the contribution is moderately novel and would be of incremental interest to experts in clinical NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: The main novel element is augmenting word-adjacency (co-occurrence) networks with subword-aware word embeddings to add semantic edges and thereby improve topological characterization of short, disfluent assessment texts; applying this enriched-network representation to MCI detection across multiple datasets/languages is a useful, concrete contribution. However, the approach largely composes established components (co-occurrence networks, word embeddings, standard classifiers, ensemble/multi-view learning) into a new pipeline rather than introducing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Given the reported cross-lingual application and handling of OOV via subword embeddings, the work represents a moderate, incremental advance rather than a highly original breakthrough.

(2016) subword embeddings, standard network topological measures, and common classifiers—but proposes a concrete enrichment of short-text networks with embedding-derived semantic edges to better represent short assessment transcripts for MCI detection. The cross-language evaluation on multiple datasets (DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, and ABCD) and the use of ensemble/multi-view fusion are useful and non-trivial extensions, but they largely integrate existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamentally new method or paradigm. Overall, the contribution is moderately novel and would be of incremental interest to experts in clinical NLP.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important clinical and NLP problem—automatic detection of MCI from speech transcripts—and proposes a lightweight, language-independent representation (complex networks enriched with word embeddings) that empirically outperforms traditional linguistic features across multiple datasets and languages (e.g., ~10% improvement on the Portuguese Cinderella set and 4.71% on the Arizona dataset, reaching 80% accuracy). By showing that embedding-enriched co-occurrence networks can better characterize short, disfluent assessment texts and that ensemble/multi-view combinations yield further gains, the work is likely to change how researchers model such data and to be built upon in related diagnostic and short-text tasks. While not necessarily transformative, the cross-dataset, multilingual evidence and practical applicability give it strong impact potential. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of automatic MCI detection from speech with a lightweight, language-independent representation (enriched word-adjacency networks), which is clinically relevant. Empirical results show the approach outperforms linguistic baselines across multiple datasets and languages (e.g., ~10% improvement on the Cinderella dataset; multi-view reached 80% on the Arizona dataset, a 4.71% gain), indicating it could change feature choices in this area. It opens promising directions for combining network topology and embeddings for short clinical texts and cross-lingual applications, though practical uptake may be tempered by limitations noted (short-text network linearity and reported accuracies of ~60–85%).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important clinical and NLP problem—automatic detection of MCI from speech transcripts—and proposes a lightweight, language-independent representation (complex networks enriched with word embeddings) that empirically outperforms traditional linguistic features across multiple datasets and languages (e.g., ~10% improvement on the Portuguese Cinderella set and 4.71% on the Arizona dataset, reaching 80% accuracy). By showing that embedding-enriched co-occurrence networks can better characterize short, disfluent assessment texts and that ensemble/multi-view combinations yield further gains, the work is likely to change how researchers model such data and to be built upon in related diagnostic and short-text tasks. While not necessarily transformative, the cross-dataset, multilingual evidence and practical applicability give it strong impact potential.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of automatic MCI detection from speech with a lightweight, language-independent representation (enriched word-adjacency networks), which is clinically relevant. Empirical results show the approach outperforms linguistic baselines across multiple datasets and languages (e.g., ~10% improvement on the Cinderella dataset; multi-view reached 80% on the Arizona dataset, a 4.71% gain), indicating it could change feature choices in this area. It opens promising directions for combining network topology and embeddings for short clinical texts and cross-lingual applications, though practical uptake may be tempered by limitations noted (short-text network linearity and reported accuracies of ~60–85%).

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology section (word-adjacency networks enriched with subword embeddings), experiments across multiple datasets, and conclusions, supporting a logical flow. Main contributions are stated early (use of enriched complex networks for MCI detection, language-independence, and classifier combination results) and the writing quality is noted as good. Notation and the presence/effectiveness of figures or tables are not described in the summary, but reporting of hyperparameters, datasets, evaluation metric (accuracy), and thorough related-work coverage indicate generally clear presentation with only minor aspects that could not be fully assessed from the summary. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from methodology to experiments and conclusions (datasets, baselines, features and classifiers are clearly described). The main contributions are stated early and explicitly (network enrichment with embeddings, cross-language evaluation, and ensemble/multi-view gains). Notation and methodological details seem consistent and well-defined — hyperparameters and multiple feature sets are reported — though the summary does not detail specific notation issues. The writing quality is described as good, but the summary does not report on the effectiveness of figures/tables/examples, so presentation there is unassessed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are generally supported by experiments across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Portuguese Cinderella, Arizona/ABCD) showing reported accuracy gains (e.g., ~10% on Cinderella, 80% on Arizona with a 4.71% improvement) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and Bag-of-Words). Mathematical content is minimal and appropriately handled; hyperparameters are reported and methodological choices (embedding enrichment, avoidance of lemmatization) are justified. However, the evaluation omits mention of statistical significance tests and code release, and the claim of language-independence is somewhat strong given only two languages/datasets—these are minor but important gaps that do not fundamentally undermine the conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main claims are supported by experiments across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Portuguese Cinderella, ABCD) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and Bag-of-Words), and the methodology (word-adjacency networks enriched with subword embeddings and topological features + standard classifiers) is technically coherent. There are no mathematical derivations to critique, but the experimental reporting omits mention of statistical significance testing, dataset sizes/class balance, and cross-validation details, which weakens confidence in the claimed improvements (e.g., "outperform linguistic features in all evaluated datasets"). The authors acknowledge relevant limitations (short-text network sparsity, OOV words) and hyperparameters are reported, so issues are minor and do not invalidate the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are generally supported by experiments across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Portuguese Cinderella, Arizona/ABCD) showing reported accuracy gains (e.g., ~10% on Cinderella, 80% on Arizona with a 4.71% improvement) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and Bag-of-Words). Mathematical content is minimal and appropriately handled; hyperparameters are reported and methodological choices (embedding enrichment, avoidance of lemmatization) are justified. However, the evaluation omits mention of statistical significance tests and code release, and the claim of language-independence is somewhat strong given only two languages/datasets—these are minor but important gaps that do not fundamentally undermine the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are supported by experiments across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Portuguese Cinderella, ABCD) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and Bag-of-Words), and the methodology (word-adjacency networks enriched with subword embeddings and topological features + standard classifiers) is technically coherent. There are no mathematical derivations to critique, but the experimental reporting omits mention of statistical significance testing, dataset sizes/class balance, and cross-validation details, which weakens confidence in the claimed improvements (e.g., "outperform linguistic features in all evaluated datasets"). The authors acknowledge relevant limitations (short-text network sparsity, OOV words) and hyperparameters are reported, so issues are minor and do not invalidate the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its approach against sensible baselines (Coh‑Metrix / Coh‑Metrix‑Dementia linguistic features and Bag‑of‑Words) across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, ABCD) using the same data and accuracy metric, and reports specific improvements with hyperparameters and related work coverage noted. It also acknowledges limitations and mixed results for the chosen embedding method. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other modern/state‑of‑the‑art MCI detection methods (e.g., recent learning‑based or representation learning baselines), so there appear to be minor omissions in competing baselines. | B(4): The paper compares its method against appropriate and relevant baselines (Coh-Metrix / Coh-Metrix-Dementia linguistic features and Bag-of-Words) across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, Arizona) using the same accuracy metric, and reports concrete improvements (e.g., ~10% on Cinderella, 4.71% on Arizona). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors analyze differences (network enrichment, short-text limitations) and report hyperparameters; related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor omission is that the summary does not mention comparison to more recent deep-learning or other state-of-the-art MCI detection methods, so the evaluation is strong but not fully exhaustive.

It also acknowledges limitations and mixed results for the chosen embedding method. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other modern/state‑of‑the‑art MCI detection methods (e.g., recent learning‑based or representation learning baselines), so there appear to be minor omissions in competing baselines.

Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors analyze differences (network enrichment, short-text limitations) and report hyperparameters; related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor omission is that the summary does not mention comparison to more recent deep-learning or other state-of-the-art MCI detection methods, so the evaluation is strong but not fully exhaustive.

main_claims: Complex network features enriched with word embeddings can be used to automatically identify Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) from speech transcripts as a lightweight and language-independent representation.
Enriching word-adjacency (co-occurrence) networks with word embeddings improves representation of short assessment texts and enables effective topological characterization for classification.

Complex network features outperform linguistic features in all evaluated datasets, and combining classifiers via ensemble and multi-view learning yields further improvements (e.g., ~10% improvement on the Cinderella dataset and multi-view achieved 80% accuracy on the Arizona dataset, a 4.71% improvement).
The proposed approach is applicable across multiple datasets and languages (English DementiaBank Cookie Theft, Portuguese Cinderella, and the ABCD/AZ-Battery dataset).
_summary.methodology: Transcripts are modeled as word-adjacency (co-occurrence) networks, enriched with word embeddings (Bojanowski et al., 2016) to add semantic similarity edges; ten network topological measures are extracted and summarized (mean, standard deviation, skewness) and used with classifiers (GaussianNB, K-NN, SVM linear/RBF, Random Forest) and combined via ensemble and multi-view learning. Bag-of-Words and linguistic metrics (Coh-Metrix / Coh-Metrix-Dementia) are used as comparative feature sets.
datasets: Arizona Battery for Communication Disorders of Dementia (ABCD)
baselines: Linguistic features (Coh-Metrix for English; Coh-Metrix-Dementia for Portuguese)
limitations: MCI is difficult to diagnose and it is not easy to distinguish healthy subjects from those with cognitive impairments (accuracies reported between ~60% and 85%).
Classification of small/short texts using networks can be impaired because short texts yield almost linear networks and topological measures may have little or no information.
Occurrence of out-of-vocabulary words in texts is common, motivating use of subword embeddings.
Lemmatization was avoided because POS tagging required for it may introduce noise/errors in disfluent transcripts.
The chosen embedding method (Bojanowski et al., 2016) provides some improvement for syntactic evaluation but not for semantic evaluation.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - detecting Mild Cognitive Impairment from speech transcripts
